title: Propagation of conditional nonclassical reservoir states during quantum decoherence
authors: Jargalsaikhan Artag, Koki Awaya, Takumi Kanezashi, Haruya Nagata, Daisuke Tsukayama, Moe Shimada, Jun-ichi Shirakashi
abstract: Decoherence is usually described as the loss of local quantum coherence after tracing over environmental degrees of freedom. This reduced description, however, hides the reservoir state that carries the lost coherence. Here we show that spin-boson decoherence can write a postselectable nonclassical imprint into a structured bosonic reservoir. We map zero- and finite-temperature reservoirs to one-dimensional chains, evolve the joint qubit-reservoir state with tensor-network dynamics, and reconstruct the Wigner function of a time-adaptive leading collective reservoir coordinate after transverse qubit readout. The conditioned mapped-reservoir coordinate develops Wigner negativity and interference fringes that are strongly suppressed in the unconditional reservoir state. A parameter sweep shows that the spectral exponent and temperature control the visibility of this conditional nonclassicality, the mapped-chain excitation transport, and the degree to which a single collective coordinate captures the imprint. These results provide a branch-resolved phase-space picture of decoherence: the reservoir is not only a sink for qubit coherence, but can carry a measurement-conditioned nonclassical state in a collective mapped coordinate.
summary: This theoretical paper explores how quantum coherence lost from a qubit during decoherence is imprinted onto the surrounding environment, or reservoir. By analyzing the reservoir state conditioned on a measurement outcome, the authors demonstrate that the environment can retain nonclassical quantum features, such as Wigner negativity, which are otherwise lost in the averaged, unconditional description.
main_problem: The paper investigates the information content carried by the environment (reservoir) during quantum decoherence, specifically contrasting the state when the system is measured (conditional) versus when it is simply traced out (unconditional).
main_result: The reservoir state, when conditioned on a specific qubit readout, develops measurable nonclassical features like Wigner negativity and interference fringes that are absent in the unconditional reservoir state.
method: The authors employ tensor-network dynamics to evolve the joint qubit-reservoir state, mapping the continuous bosonic bath to a discrete 1D chain representation for analysis.
model_or_system: The model is based on spin-boson decoherence, coupling a two-level system (qubit) to a structured bosonic reservoir. The dynamics are analyzed by mapping the bath to a 1D chain Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Wigner negativity ($	ext{Vnc}$), qubit coherence ($\langle\sigma_xangle$), reservoir occupation ($\langle c^\dagger_k c_k angle$), and the conditional/unconditional reservoir state.
important_parameters: Spectral exponent ($s$), temperature ($\Theta/\Delta$), and coupling strengths ($\alpha$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mapping the continuous bosonic bath to a finite 1D chain, which is an approximation for the full reservoir dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mapping from the spin-boson geometry to the 1D chain, parameter landscapes showing peak conditional nonclassicality, and time evolution plots comparing conditional vs. unconditional reservoir properties.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the model and mapping, then calculating and comparing the conditional and unconditional reservoir states using Wigner functions, and finally analyzing how temperature and spectral properties control the visibility of this conditional nonclassical imprint.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed, phase-space picture of decoherence, showing that the environment acts as a memory that retains measurement-dependent quantum information, which is crucial for understanding quantum measurement processes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Spin-boson reservoir and mapped-chain representation. A qubit coupled longitudinally to a structured bosonic reservoir is mapped from the star geometry to an equivalent one-dimensional chain. The qubit couples directly to the first chain coordinate, while the remaining chain modes transport reservoir excitation away from the system. The chain coordinate is a mapped spectral-reservoir coordinate, not a real-space coordinate.

FIG. 2. Parameter landscape for conditional reservoir nonclassicality. Sweep over coupling α, spectral exponent s, and temperature Θ/∆. The panels show peak conditional Wigner nonclassicality, qubit coherence loss at the same event, and postselection probability for zero temperature (top row) and finite temperature (bottom row). White boxes mark the four main cases used throughout this study: α = 0.2, s = 0.5 or s = 1.0, and Θ/∆= 0 or 1.

FIG. 3. Four-case qubit dynamics. Time evolution of (a) signed qubit coherence ⟨σx⟩, (b) qubit purity, and (c) qubit entropy in the pure mapped-chain representation, S(ρS). Insets in (b) and (c) zoom the near-maximal-mixing window; the reference values are Tr ρ2 S = 1/2 and ln 2, respectively. The sign of ⟨σx⟩retains phase information.

FIG. 4. Reservoir excitation transport in the mapped chain. Mapped-chain occupation ⟨c† kck⟩for the four selected cases. Panels (a-d) show s = 0.5, Θ/∆= 0; s = 0.5, Θ/∆= 1; s = 1.0, Θ/∆= 0; and s = 1.0, Θ/∆= 1, respectively, all at α = 0.2. The excitation generated during decoherence moves away from the qubit in the mapped chain coordinate. The dashed white line is the hopping-scale guide k = vguidet, with vguide = 2 maxn |tn|.

Figure 3(a-c) shows the qubit dynamics for the four selected cases. The signed coherence ⟨σx⟩starts at one and decays rapidly as system-bath entanglement forms. When ⟨σx⟩becomes negative, the transverse Bloch com- ponent has changed phase relative to the initial +x direc- tion; this sign change is not a change in the sign of a decay rate. The purity approaches the maximally mixed value near the strongest entangling part of the dynamics, while the qubit entropy S(ρS) rises toward ln 2, confirming that coherence loss is accompanied by strong system-reservoir correlation in the pure mapped-chain representation.
